input.headline: Nasa releases new images of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS

raw.headline: Nasa releases new images of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS

winner: NASA released the new images of comet 3I/ATLAS, but they had to blur the background because the comet was technically trespassing in another galaxy.

candidates.text: Why did the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS refuse to go to NASA's party? Because it was *too out of this world*!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Pun on 'out of this world' is clever and specific to the comet's interstellar nature; concise and surprising."}

candidates.text: NASA is really getting space-y with these new images of 3I/ATLAS, but honestly, I think they're just trying to comet-ment to the bit.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Creative wordplay with 'comet-ment to the bit' is clever and specific, aligns well with the headline, and uses natural English without being overly complex."}

candidates.text: NASA released new images of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS — turns out, it’s just a cosmic tourist taking a selfie.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and specific, using a relatable metaphor ('cosmic tourist taking a selfie') that adds humor while staying relevant to the headline."}

candidates.text: Nasa released a new image of Comet 3I/ATLAS—turns out, it wasn't a comet at all. It was just a very disappointed cloud that gave up on space.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and specific to the headline. It uses personification humor effectively with 'disappointed cloud,' which adds a whimsical twist."}

candidates.text: NASA released new photos of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS, and now everyone’s asking if it's a dating app for aliens.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Creative twist on 'dating app' with aliens, ties to headline, concise and surprising."}

candidates.text: NASA released the new images of comet 3I/ATLAS, but they had to blur the background because the comet was technically trespassing in another galaxy.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, surprising, and specific, using a playful take on 'trespassing' in a scientific context."}

candidates.text: Nasa released the new images and immediately got a cease-and-desist letter from the Interstellar Comet Owners' Association—turns out 3I/ATLAS was actually just a space condominium.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist with a specific reference to a fictional organization, concise and funny with natural language."}

candidates.text: NASA released new images of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS — turns out it wasn’t looking for a job, just passing through.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is concise, uses a play on words ('looking for a job' vs. 'passing through'), and ties into the comet's interstellar nature. It’s slightly generic but still surprising and specific to the topic."}

candidates.text: NASA released stunning new images of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS, proving that while it may be a wandering traveler from another galaxy, it still hasn't figured out how to do its own laundry.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 7.5, "reason": "The joke is creative and surprising, using a relatable analogy (laundry) to contrast with the comet's cosmic nature. It is concise and specific, though the humor relies on a mild pun that may not resonate as strongly with all audiences."}
